old_claim.note: Public Key Infrastructure provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Electronic health records digitize the older patient-record lineage; the Regenstrief system is described as a computer-based medical record for maintaining patient records.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract description of the Regenstrief Medical Record system as a computer-based medical record system for ambulatory care in operation since 1973.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Regenstrief article describes a computer-based medical record system containing core medical-record data and addressing efficient use and maintenance of patient records.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports the electronic-record lineage from patient records to computer-based records; it does not support PKI as an early prerequisite because secure public-key infrastructure is absent from the 1973 system description.

ontology_before: Electronic health records depended on public-key infrastructure, projecting later secure-exchange infrastructure onto early computer-based medical records.
ontology_after: Electronic health records are modeled as digitized patient records enabled by hospital information systems and data-management infrastructure; PKI is later exchange/security context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electronic_health_records no longer directly depends on public_key_infrastructure and now preserves the medical-case-record lineage.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped specifically to internet-scale secure EHR exchange rather than electronic records as a technology.
A source showed the 1973 Regenstrief computer-based medical record required public-key infrastructure.
The graph reintroduced public_key_infrastructure as a direct prerequisite for early EHRs.
short_reason: PKI is later security infrastructure; EHRs are the digitized continuation of patient records.
